title: Chapter 23: The Ghost
content: The tower room smells like dust and old paper and something else — something chemical, like dried ink that's been baking in summer heat for years. The stairs creak under my weight and each one sounds like a confession. I count them. Twenty-two. I counted them before, the last time I came up here, but I've forgotten the number already. That's the thing about this house. It makes you forget what matters.
Not this time.

I need Peter's original documents. The USB drive Mrs. Grose gave me has copies — filtered, organized, digitized into something clean and navigable. Peter wouldn't have kept clean copies. Peter was a man who built mazes. The source would be here, in the room where he built them.

The door is unlocked. It was locked before. I'm certain of that. But the latch gives when I turn it, and the door swings inward with a sigh, and I tell myself that houses settle, that locks fail, that this doesn't mean anything. I tell myself that as I step inside.

The tower room is exactly as I remember it: the desk by the arrow-slit window, the bookshelves bowed under the weight of unmarked binders, the leather chair cracked and cratered like a face after fire. Afternoons slant through the narrow window and fall across the desk in a blade of light, and the dust spins in the current of my breathing, and I can taste it — dry, mineral, the flavor of things left undisturbed.
I start with the desk.

The top drawer is unlocked. Pens. Pencils. A compass with a broken arm. The second drawer sticks, and I have to wiggle it, and when it opens there are graph-paper notebooks stacked three deep. I flip one open. Peter's handwriting — tight, precise, slanting forward as though the words were running. *Round One: Isolation. Goal: sever external dependencies. Reward: information. Penalty: silence.* Dated March 2010. The twins would have been seven.
I set the notebook aside. Open another. *Round Two: Trust. Goal: identify the ally. The ally is the person who lies to you least. Reward: alliance. Penalty: suspicion.* April 2010.
Seven years old, and Peter was already teaching them that trust was a mechanism.

The bottom drawer is locked. A small brass padlock, tarnished green at the edges. I look at it for a long moment. Then I reach up and pull the hairpin from my bun — the one thing in this house I brought with me, the one thing that's mine — and I bend it, and I slide it into the lock, and I feel for the pins.
I've never picked a lock before. The hairpin moves like it knows what it's doing. Like I know what it's doing. The lock clicks open.
There's a part of me that's not surprised at all.
Inside the drawer: more notebooks, older, their covers soft with handling. And beneath them, an envelope. Cream-colored, foxed with age, unsealed. My name is not on it.
*Caroline* is on it.
My mother's name.

I don't pick up the envelope. I stare at it. The light from the window hits the paper and I can see where fingers — his fingers — folded it, refolded it, creased it with indecision. He never sent it. The envelope has no stamp, no postmark. Just Caroline, in Peter's running hand, and the ghosts of fingertips pressed into the paper like bruises.
I pick it up.
The letter is two pages, written on both sides in blue ink that has faded to the color of lake water. I sit in the cracked leather chair and the dust puffs around me and I read.
*Caroline,*
*If you're reading this, I'm dead. I know that's a coward's way to begin. But I was a coward in all the ways that mattered and brave in none of them, so at least I'm consistent.*
*I need to tell you about the children. Both of them. And I need to tell you about the one you took with you.*
*Maeve.*

*I've never written her name before. It looks strange on the page. It looks like something I don't deserve to write. I gave her nothing — not my name, not my attention, not the knowledge that I existed. And still she exists, and she is mine in the only way that matters: she carries whatever sickness made me. I see it already, in the reports. She watches. She calculates. She survives. She learned the game before I ever taught it to her, because she is my daughter, and the game is what I am.*

*I built something terrible, Caroline. I built it because I didn't know how to love. The children — they're not my children, but I made them in my image. I designed the game the way a man designs a prison: with admiration for the architecture and no thought for the inmates. It teaches them to survive, but it doesn't teach them to live. I know that now. I knew it then. I built it anyway.*

*The game is elegant. I'm proud of it, and that pride is the ugliest thing about me. It works because people work — because we betray each other in predictable ways, because we want things we can't name, because we'd rather win than be known. I built a machine that turns those qualities into fuel, and it runs and runs and it will never stop, Caroline, because I built it too well.*
*I don't know how to stop it now. Maybe I never did. Maybe that's why I didn't try. It's easier to admire an engine than to dismantle it.*

*If you're reading this, I'm dead. And the game will continue without me. Unless someone changes the rules. That's what I'm afraid of, and that's what I'm hoping for. The same thing. Someone who is like me — someone who understands the machine well enough to break it. Someone who will choose to break it.*
*But the machine makes players, Caroline. Not breakers. And anyone who understands it well enough to end it will understand it well enough to play it better than I ever did.*
*That's the paradox. That's the trap. That's my daughter.*
*Don't let her be me.*
*Peter*
I read it three times.

The first time, I'm looking for the manipulation. The strategy. The move. Peter was a game designer — everything he wrote was a move, every sentence a positioning of pieces. So I read it like a player, checking for the angle, the setup, the trap door beneath the apparent confession. And I find it: *Don't let her be me.* That's the hook. That's the thing designed to make the reader — to make Caroline, to make me — feel the gravity of his regret. A monster who knows he's a monster is still a monster. But a monster who knows he's a monster and asks you to stop him — that's a different kind of trap. That's a trap where your sympathy is the bait.

The second time, I read it like his daughter. And I see the shape of him for the first time — not the architect of the Game, not the figure in the photograph, not the absent ghost who haunts this house more thoroughly than any widow, but a man. A man who built something he couldn't control. A man who loved the thing he made more than the people it consumed. A man who sat in this chair, in this tower, and wrote to a woman he'd abandoned, and confessed, and never sent the letter because sending it would have meant the confession was real.
The leather chair creaks under me. The dust settles. The tower holds its breath.

The third time, I read it like myself. And I see the thing he was afraid of. *Anyone who understands it well enough to end it will understand it well enough to play it better than I ever did.* He saw me before I existed. He predicted the player I'd become. And he was afraid — not that I'd fail, but that I'd succeed. That I'd learn the Game so completely that destroying it would feel like a waste. Like dismantling a clock when you could just keep it running.
He was afraid I'd be him.

The letter trembles in my hands. I'm not cold. The tower is warm, suffocatingly warm, the afternoon light thick as honey in the narrow room. My hands are shaking because my body understands something my mind hasn't accepted yet: Peter Quint was my father. Not in the way the twins claim him — not through the Game, not through belonging to the house — but in blood, in genetics, in whatever vile inheritance passes from the person who made you to the person you become.

I have his mind. I picked a lock with a hairpin on my first try. I moved through this house like it was designed for me, because maybe it was. I understood the Game before I knew it existed, because the Game is how Peter thought, and Peter's thoughts are the water I swim in.
The question isn't whether I can destroy the Game.
The question is whether I should.

If the Game ends, what happens to the twins? They've been shaped by it — every impulse, every alliance, every betrayal calibrated by Peter's rules. Without the Game, who are they? What's left when you strip away the framework that made you? I know the answer because I'm asking the same question about myself. Strip away the Game and I'm just Peter's daughter, standing in his tower, holding his letter, thinking his thoughts.
"You found his letter."

The voice comes from the doorway and I don't flinch. I don't jump. I turn my head slowly, the way a person turns when they already know what they'll see, and Miles is standing in the frame of the door with one hand on the jamb and the other at his side, and he's watching me the way I've seen him watch the lake — still, patient, waiting for something to surface.

His breathing is unsteady. I can hear it from here, the slight hitch on the inhale, the controlled exhale. He's been running. Or he's been standing there longer than I think and the effort of staying silent has worn him down.
"Did you know about this?" I hold up the letter.
"Yes."
The word drops between us like a stone in water. No ripples. Just the sink.

"He wrote it the night before he died. He never sent it." Miles doesn't move from the doorway. His face is the mask — the one I've seen since the first day, the smooth porcelain composure that makes him look older than seventeen and younger than a person — but something's wrong with it. The edges aren't aligned. The corners of his mouth are held too precisely, like a painting that's been retouched. "I found it when I was twelve. I came up here looking for — I don't remember what. Something of his. Anything. I found the letter instead."
"It's why you started playing for real."

He doesn't confirm it. He doesn't have to. I can see it in the way his jaw tightens, the way his fingers press white against the doorframe. He was twelve. He'd just lost the only father he'd known, and he found a letter that said *the game will continue without me*, and he heard an instruction where Peter had written a confession.

"He wanted me to stop," Miles says. His voice is very quiet. The mask is cracking — not breaking, not yet, but fracturing along lines I've never seen, hairline fractures that let something raw and adolescent leak through. "But stopping felt like letting him down. The game was the only thing he left us."
"He didn't leave you the game. He left you a warning."

"Same thing." And now Miles smiles, and it's the worst thing I've seen in this house, worse than the lake at night, worse than Flora's glassy calm, worse than my own face in the bathroom mirror that first week. Because it's real. "He warned us because he knew we wouldn't listen. That's the game, Maeve. The warning is Round One. The rule you're supposed to break."

The dust settles around us. The tower is so still I can hear the house breathing — pipes and wood and the distant machinery of the kitchen below. Miles's eyes are on me and they are bright and tired and seventeen and ancient.
"You want to destroy the game," he says. It isn't a question.
"Fine. But you'll have to go through me."
"I don't want to go through you, Miles."
"Then you've already lost."

He says it without cruelty. That's what makes it land. He's not threatening me. He's stating a fact, the way you'd note the time or the weather. *If you won't go through me, you've already lost.* And the terrible thing is that he's right. The Game teaches you to go through people. It teaches you that alliance is a strategy, not a feeling. It teaches you that the only move that matters is the next one. And I don't want to go through him. I don't want to treat Miles like a piece on the board.
Which means I've already lost.

He turns to leave. His hand leaves the doorframe and his shoulder rotates and his body begins the motion of walking away, and I think he's going to go, I think that's the exit, I think the scene is over and I'm left here with the letter and the silence and the dust.
Then he stops. Half-turned. His profile against the tower's dim light, the arrow-slit window painting a blade of gold across his cheek.
"She really did walk into the lake, you know."
My chest tightens. "What?"

"Rebecca. She walked into the lake. I didn't push her." His voice is flat. Rehearsed. Like he's said it before — to himself, to the dark, to whatever tribunal meets in the hours before dawn. "I just didn't stop her. There's a difference."
"Is there?"

He doesn't answer. He stands in the doorway with his back half to me and the light on his face and the silence filling up the room like water, and I think he's going to say something else — I can see his throat working, the swallow that doesn't become a word — but he doesn't. He walks away. His footsteps descend the stairs, one after another, and I count them. Twenty-two. And then nothing.

I sit in Peter's chair in Peter's tower with Peter's letter in my left hand and I reach into my pocket with my right and I close my fingers around the USB drive Mrs. Grose gave me. Two artifacts. Two truths.

The letter says Peter wanted the Game to end. He was afraid of what it would do to his daughter — to me — if it continued. He wrote a confession and a warning and he sealed it in an envelope and he locked it in a drawer and he died the next day without sending it, because even at the end he couldn't bring himself to finish the move. He could see the trap but he couldn't walk out of it.

The drive says the Game will never end on its own. The files show the architecture: self-repairing, self-generating, every broken rule replaced by two new ones, every exit a corridor that curves back to the center. Peter built something that outlived him because he built it to. The machine runs on the people inside it, and there will always be people inside it, because the machine makes sure of that.
I have to choose.

Honor my father's last wish — end the Game, even if it means dismantling the only structure the twins have ever known, even if it means entering the machine so deeply I might not find my way out. Destroy it and hope that what's left after is a person and not just wreckage.

Or become my father's last player — learn the Game so completely that I can play it from the inside, shape it, bend it, keep it running but point it somewhere else. Not destruction. Redirection. The way Peter might have done if he'd been braver. The way Peter was afraid to try.

The letter crinkles in my fist. The drive presses its rectangular shape into my palm. The tower holds its breath and the house breathes below me and somewhere in it Miles is walking through a corridor, and Flora is sitting in a room I can't see, and Mrs. Grose is polishing something that's already clean, and the Game is running. It is always running.
I am Peter's daughter. I understand the machine well enough to break it.
I am Peter's daughter. I understand the machine well enough to play it better than he ever did.
For the first time since I came to Bly Manor, I am not sure which one I want to be.

The dust settles. The light shifts. The tower keeps my secret, and the stairs wait for my feet, and the house goes on breathing, and the Game — the Game, the Game, the beautiful, terrible, endless Game — doesn't care what I choose.
It only cares that I play.
